From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Subclass of energy crisis

1970s energy crisis

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Real and nominal price of oil, 1968–2006.[[1]](#cite_note-1) | |
| Date | 1973 (1973)–1980 (1980) |
| Also known as | 1970s oil crisis |

The **1970s energy crisis** occurred when the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), particularly the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), faced substantial [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") shortages as well as elevated prices. The two worst crises of this period were the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") and the [1979 oil crisis](/wiki/1979_oil_crisis "1979 oil crisis"), when, respectively, the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War") and the [Iranian Revolution](/wiki/Iranian_Revolution "Iranian Revolution") triggered interruptions in [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") oil exports.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The crisis began to unfold as petroleum production in the United States and some other parts of the world peaked in the late 1960s and early 1970s.[[3]](#cite_note-mkinghubbert1956-3) World oil production per capita began a long-term decline after 1979.[[4]](#cite_note-RCDuncan2001-4) The oil crises prompted the first shift towards energy-saving (in particular, fossil fuel-saving) technologies.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The major industrial centers of the world were forced to contend with escalating issues related to petroleum supply. Western countries relied on the resources of countries in the Middle East and other parts of the world. The crisis led to stagnant economic growth in many countries as oil prices surged.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Although there were genuine concerns with supply, part of the run-up in prices resulted from the perception of a crisis. The combination of stagnant growth and price inflation during this era led to the coinage of the term *[stagflation](/wiki/Stagflation "Stagflation")*.[[7]](#cite_note-7) By the 1980s, both the recessions of the 1970s and adjustments in local economies to become more efficient in petroleum usage, controlled demand sufficiently for petroleum prices worldwide to return to more sustainable levels.

The period was not uniformly negative for all economies. Petroleum-rich countries in the Middle East benefited from increased prices and the slowing production in other areas of the world. Some other countries, such as Norway, Mexico, and Venezuela, benefited as well. In the United States, [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), as well as some other oil-producing areas, experienced major economic booms due to soaring oil prices even as most of the rest of the nation struggled with the stagnant economy. Many of these economic gains, however, came to a halt as prices stabilized and dropped in the 1980s.

## Key periods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Key periods")]

### Arab-Israeli conflict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Arab-Israeli conflict")]

[Conflict between Arabs and Israelis](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict") in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") has existed since [Israel's](/wiki/Israel "Israel") declaration of independence in 1948, including [a number of wars](/wiki/List_of_wars_involving_Israel "List of wars involving Israel"). The [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis"), also known as the Second Arab–Israeli war, was sparked when Israel's southern port of [Eilat](/wiki/Port_of_Eilat "Port of Eilat") [was blocked by Egypt](/wiki/Israeli_passage_through_the_Suez_Canal_and_Straits_of_Tiran "Israeli passage through the Suez Canal and Straits of Tiran"), which also [nationalized](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") belonging to Anglo-French investors. One of the objectives of the invasion was the removal of President [Gamal Abdel Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser") who was aligning with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War") of 1967 included an Israeli invasion of the Egyptian [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), which resulted in Egypt's [closure of the Suez Canal for eight years](/wiki/Closure_of_the_Suez_Canal_(1967-1975) "Closure of the Suez Canal (1967-1975)") after [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") occupied the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"). The canal was [cleared in 1974](/wiki/1974_Suez_Canal_Clearance_Operation "1974 Suez Canal Clearance Operation") and opened again in 1975[[9]](#cite_note-9) after the 1973 [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War"), when Egypt tried and failed to take back the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) [OAPEC](/wiki/OAPEC "OAPEC") countries cut production of oil and placed an [embargo](/wiki/Embargo "Embargo") on oil exports to the United States after [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") requested $2.2 billion to support Israel in the war after learning that the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), was aiding [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") in the war. Nevertheless, the embargo lasted only until January 1974, though the price of oil remained high afterwards.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Production peaks around 1970

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production peaks around 1970")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/US_Crude_Oil_Production_and_Imports.svg/250px-US_Crude_Oil_Production_and_Imports.svg.png)](/wiki/File:US_Crude_Oil_Production_and_Imports.svg)

United States oil production and imports, showing a production peak in 1970

Further information: [Peak oil](/wiki/Peak_oil "Peak oil")

The real price of petroleum was stable in the 1970 timeframe, but there had been a sharp increase in American imports, putting a strain on American balance of trade, alongside other developed nations. During the 1960s, petroleum production in some of the world's top producers with extraction technology at the time began to peak. [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") reached its production peak in 1966, [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") and the United States in 1970, and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") in 1974.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")'s conventional oil production peaked around the same time (though non-conventional production later helped revive Canadian production to some degree).[[15]](#cite_note-15) The worldwide production per capita peaked soon afterward.[[4]](#cite_note-RCDuncan2001-4)

Although production in other parts of the world was increasing, the peaks in these regions began to put substantial upward pressure on world oil prices. Equally as important, control of the oil supply became an increasingly important problem as countries like West Germany and the U.S. became increasingly dependent on foreign suppliers for this key resource.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### 1973 oil crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1973 oil crisis")]

Main article: [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis")

The 1973 oil crisis was a direct consequence of the U.S production peak in late 1960 and the beginning of 1971 (and shortages, especially for heating oil, started from there). The "embargo" as described below is the "practical name" given to the crisis. For the main Arab producers, the "embargo" supposedly allowed them to show to "the [Arab street](/wiki/Arab_street "Arab street")" that they were doing something for the Palestinians. In reality, it was more a desire to punish the U.S for re-supplying [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") with weapons during the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War"), after the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") re-supplied [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") with weapons. In real market terms (number of barrels) the embargo was almost a non-event, and only from a few countries, towards a few countries.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The embargo was never effective from Saudi Arabia towards the US, as reported by [James E. Akins](/wiki/James_E._Akins "James E. Akins") in interview at 24:10 in the documentary "la face cachée du pétrole part 2".[[18]](#cite_note-18) Akins, who audited US capacity for Nixon after US peak, was US ambassador in Saudi Arabia at that time.
[Lawrence Rocks](/wiki/Lawrence_Rocks_(chemist) "Lawrence Rocks (chemist)") and Richard Runyon captured the unfolding of these events at the time in The Energy Crisis book.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)
In October 1973, the members of [Organization of Arab Petroleum Exporting Countries](/wiki/Organization_of_Arab_Petroleum_Exporting_Countries "Organization of Arab Petroleum Exporting Countries") or the OAPEC (consisting of the [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") members of [OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC")) proclaimed an oil [embargo](/wiki/Embargo "Embargo") "in response to the U.S. decision to re-supply the Israeli military" during the [Yom Kippur war](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War"), after the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") re-supplied [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"); it lasted until March 1974.[[21]](#cite_note-USstate2ndCrisis-21) OAPEC declared it would limit or stop [oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") shipments to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and other countries if they supported [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") in the conflict. With the US actions seen as initiating the oil embargo, the long-term possibility of embargo-related high oil prices, disrupted supply and recession, created a strong rift within [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"); both European countries and Japan sought to disassociate themselves from the US Middle East policy. Arab oil producers had also linked the end of the embargo with successful US efforts to create peace in the Middle East, which complicated the situation. To address these developments, the [Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") Administration began parallel negotiations with both Arab oil producers to end the embargo, and with [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), and Israel to arrange an Israeli pull back from the Sinai and the Golan Heights after the fighting stopped. By January 18, 1974, [Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") [Henry Kissinger](/wiki/Henry_Kissinger "Henry Kissinger") had negotiated an Israeli troop withdrawal from parts of the Sinai. The promise of a negotiated settlement between Israel and Syria was sufficient to convince Arab oil producers to lift the embargo in March 1974. By May, Israel agreed to withdraw from parts of the [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights") [[21]](#cite_note-USstate2ndCrisis-21) close to the Syrian border.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Oil_Prices_Since_1861.svg/500px-Oil_Prices_Since_1861.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Oil_Prices_Since_1861.svg)

Graph of oil prices from 1861 to 2007, showing a sharp increase in 1973, and again in 1979. The orange line is [adjusted for inflation](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics) "Real versus nominal value (economics)").

Independently, the OPEC members agreed to use their leverage over the world price-setting mechanism for [oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") to stabilize their real incomes by raising world oil prices. This action followed several years of steep income declines after the recent failure of negotiations with the major Western oil companies earlier in the month.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For the most part, industrialized economies relied on crude oil,[[22]](#cite_note-22) and OPEC was a major supplier.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Because of the dramatic inflation experienced during this period, a popular economic theory has been that these price increases were to blame, as being suppressive of economic activity. However, the causality stated by this theory is often questioned.[[24]](#cite_note-Barsky-24) The targeted countries responded with a wide variety of new, and mostly permanent, initiatives to contain their further dependency. The 1973 "oil price shock", along with the [1973–1974 stock market crash](/wiki/1973%E2%80%931974_stock_market_crash "1973–1974 stock market crash"), have been regarded as the first event since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") to have a persistent economic effect.[[25]](#cite_note-Perron1988-25)

### 1979 energy crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1979 energy crisis")]

Main article: [1979 oil crisis](/wiki/1979_oil_crisis "1979 oil crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Top_Oil_Producing_Countries.png/250px-Top_Oil_Producing_Countries.png)](/wiki/File:Top_Oil_Producing_Countries.png)

Production in top countries by year (million barrels per day)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

A crisis emerged in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in 1979 during the wake of the [Iranian Revolution](/wiki/Iranian_Revolution "Iranian Revolution"). Amid massive protests, the [Shah of Iran](/wiki/Pahlavi_dynasty "Pahlavi dynasty"), [Mohammad Reza Pahlavi](/wiki/Mohammad_Reza_Pahlavi "Mohammad Reza Pahlavi"), fled his country in early 1979, allowing the [Ayatollah](/wiki/Ayatollah "Ayatollah") [Khomeini](/wiki/Ruhollah_Khomeini "Ruhollah Khomeini") to gain control. The protests shattered the Iranian oil sector. While the new regime resumed oil exports, it was inconsistent and at a lower volume, forcing prices to go up. [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and other [OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC") nations, under the presidency of [Dr. Mana Alotaiba](/wiki/Dr._Mana_Alotaiba "Dr. Mana Alotaiba") increased production to offset the decline, and the overall loss in production was about 4 percent.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, a widespread panic resulted, driving the price far higher than would be expected under normal circumstances.

In 1980, following the [Iraqi invasion of Iran](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War#1980:_Iraqi_invasion "Iran–Iraq War"), oil production in Iran nearly stopped, and Iraq's oil production was severely cut as well.

After 1980, oil prices began a decline as other countries began to fill the production shortfalls from Iran and Iraq.

### 1980s oil glut

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1980s oil glut")]

Main article: [1980s oil glut](/wiki/1980s_oil_glut "1980s oil glut")

The [1973](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") and [1979 energy crisis](/wiki/1979_energy_crisis "1979 energy crisis") had caused petroleum prices to peak in 1980 at over [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")35 per barrel (US$137 in today's[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] dollars). Following these events slowing industrial economies and stabilization of supply and demand caused prices to begin falling in the 1980s.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The glut began in the early 1980s as a result of slowed economic activity in industrial countries (due to the 1973 and 1979 energy crises) and the [energy conservation](/wiki/Energy_conservation "Energy conservation") spurred by high fuel prices.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") adjusted [real 2004 dollar value](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics) "Real versus nominal value (economics)") of oil fell from an average of $78.2 per barrel in 1981 to an average of $26.8 in 1986.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In June 1981, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* stated an "Oil glut! ... is here"[[31]](#cite_note-31) and *[Time Magazine](/wiki/Time_Magazine "Time Magazine")* stated: "the world temporarily floats in a glut of oil",[[32]](#cite_note-32) though the next week a New York Times article warned that the word "glut" was misleading, and that in reality, while temporary surpluses had brought down prices somewhat, prices were still well above pre-energy crisis levels.[[33]](#cite_note-33) This sentiment was echoed in November 1981, when the CEO of [Exxon](/wiki/Exxon "Exxon") also characterized the glut as a temporary surplus, and that the word "glut" was an example of "our American penchant for exaggerated language". He wrote that the main cause of the glut was declining consumption. In the United States, Europe and Japan, oil consumption had fallen 13% from 1979 to 1981, due to "in part, in reaction to the very large increases in oil prices by the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries and other oil exporters", continuing a trend begun during the 1973 price increases.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

After 1980, reduced demand and overproduction produced a glut on the world market, causing a six-year-long decline in oil prices culminating with a 46 percent price drop in 1986.

## Effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Effects")]

### Recession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Recession")]

Main article: [1973–75 recession](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9375_recession "1973–75 recession")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/1973-75_recession.jpg/330px-1973-75_recession.jpg)](/wiki/File:1973-75_recession.jpg)

In the parlance of [recession shapes](/wiki/Recession_shapes "Recession shapes"), the Recession of 1973–75 in the United States could be considered a [U-shaped recession](/wiki/U-shaped_recession "U-shaped recession"), because of its prolonged period of weak growth and contraction.[[35]](#cite_note-TBM-35)  
  Percent change from preceding period in real gross domestic product (annualized; seasonally adjusted)  
  Average GDP growth 1947–2009  
Source: [Bureau of Economic Analysis](/wiki/Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis "Bureau of Economic Analysis")

The U.S. reported a negative economic growth during the period concerning the 1970s and it remained weak till the 1980s as the post World War II economic boom drew to a close. But it was a different type of recession as it was a scenario of stagflation which is a rare economic consequence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Other causes that contributed to the recession included the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), which turned out costly for the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the fall of the [Bretton Woods system](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_system "Bretton Woods system"). The emergence of [newly industrialized countries](/wiki/Newly_industrialized_countries "Newly industrialized countries") rose competition in the metal industry, triggering a [steel crisis](/wiki/Steel_crisis "Steel crisis"), where industrial core areas in North America and Europe were forced to re-structure. The [1973–1974 stock market crash](/wiki/1973%E2%80%931974_stock_market_crash "1973–1974 stock market crash") made the recession evident.

According to the [National Bureau of Economic Research](/wiki/National_Bureau_of_Economic_Research "National Bureau of Economic Research"), the U.S. economy slid into a recession during the period of 1973–75.[[36]](#cite_note-NBER-36) Inflation levels remained high even when an economic expansion took place afterwards.

During this recession, the [Gross Domestic Product](/wiki/Gross_Domestic_Product "Gross Domestic Product") of the United States fell 3.2%. Although the recession ended in March 1975, the unemployment rate did not peak for several months. In May 1975, the rate reached its height for the cycle of 9%.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The recession also lasted from 1973 to 1975 in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The GDP declined by 3.9%[[38]](#cite_note-ir09feb-38)[[39]](#cite_note-ONS09Feb-39) or 3.37%[[40]](#cite_note-40) depending on the source. It took 14 quarters for the UK's GDP to recover to that at the start of recession.[[38]](#cite_note-ir09feb-38)

### Emergence of new oil producers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Emergence of new oil producers")]

High oil prices in the 1970s induced investment in oil production by non-OPEC countries, particularly for reserves with a higher cost of production.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) These included [Prudhoe Bay](/wiki/Prudhoe_Bay_Oil_Field "Prudhoe Bay Oil Field") in Alaska, the [North Sea offshore fields](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") of the United Kingdom and Norway, the Cantarell offshore field of Mexico, and [oil sands](/wiki/Oil_sands "Oil sands") in Canada.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Strategic petroleum reserves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Strategic petroleum reserves")]

Main article: [Global strategic petroleum reserves](/wiki/Global_strategic_petroleum_reserves "Global strategic petroleum reserves")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/US_Strategic_Petroleum_Reserve.webp/500px-US_Strategic_Petroleum_Reserve.webp.png)](/wiki/File:US_Strategic_Petroleum_Reserve.webp)

United States [Strategic Petroleum Reserve](/wiki/Strategic_Petroleum_Reserve_(United_States) "Strategic Petroleum Reserve (United States)")   
 Weekly data points since 1982   

[Barrels of oil](/wiki/Barrel_(unit) "Barrel (unit)")

As a result of the [1973 crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") many nations created strategic petroleum reserves (SPRs), crude oil inventories (or stockpiles) held by the governments of particular countries or private industry, for the purpose of providing economic and national security during an energy crisis. The [International Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") (IEA) was formed in the wake of this crisis and currently comprises 31 member countries.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) According to the IEA, approximately 4.1 billion barrels (650,000,000 m3) of oil are held in strategic reserves by the member countries, of which 1.4 billion barrels (220,000,000 m3) is government-controlled. The remainder is held by private industry.[[48]](#cite_note-48) These reserves are intended to be equivalent to at least 90 days of net imports. At the moment the [U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve](/wiki/Strategic_Petroleum_Reserve_(United_States) "Strategic Petroleum Reserve (United States)") is one of the largest government-owned reserves, with a capacity of up to 713.5 million barrels (113,440,000 m3).[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Recently, other non-IEA countries have begun creating their own strategic petroleum reserves, with China being the second largest overall and the largest non-IEA country.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Middle East")]

Further information: [Arab–Israeli conflict](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict")

Since [Israel's declaration of independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_(Israel) "Declaration of Independence (Israel)") in 1948 this state has found itself in nearly continual conflict with the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") and some other predominantly [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") countries. The animosity between the Arabs and the Israelis became a global issue during the 1970s. The [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War") of 1973, with the supplying of Israel by its Western allies while some Arab states received [Soviet](/wiki/USSR "USSR") supplies, made this one of the most internationally threatening confrontations of the period.

The large oil discoveries in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") gave some Muslim countries unique leverage in the world, beginning in the 1960s. The 1973 and 1979 crises, in particular, were demonstrations of the new power that these countries had found. The United States and other countries were forced to become more involved in the conflicts between these states and Israel.

### OPEC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: OPEC")]

Further information: [OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC")

One of the first challenges OPEC faced in the 1970s was the United States' [unilaterally pulling out](/wiki/Nixon_Shock "Nixon Shock") of the [Bretton Woods Accord](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_system "Bretton Woods system") and taking the U.S. off the established [Gold Exchange Standard](/wiki/Gold_Exchange_Standard "Gold Exchange Standard") in 1971. The change resulted in instability in world currencies and [depreciation](/wiki/Depreciation_(currency) "Depreciation (currency)") of the value of the [U.S. dollar](/wiki/U.S._dollar "U.S. dollar"), as well as other currencies. The revenues of OPEC also took a hit since they priced oil in dollars.

Finally OPEC started pricing oil against gold to combat the situation.[[51]](#cite_note-IndepBlackGold-51)
But OPEC still struggled to maintain stability in the region as the negotiations between them and other Western oil companies bore little to no positive results.

### "Oil Patch"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970s_energy_crisis&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: \"Oil Patch\"")]

The major oil-producing regions of the U.S.—[Texas](/wiki/Energy_in_Texas#Fossil_fuel_production "Energy in Texas"), [Oklahoma](/wiki/Energy_in_Oklahoma "Energy in Oklahoma"), [Louisiana](/wiki/Energy_in_Louisiana "Energy in Louisiana"), [Colorado](/wiki/Energy_in_Colorado "Energy in Colorado"), [Wyoming](/wiki/Energy_in_Wyoming "Energy in Wyoming"), and [Alaska](/wiki/Energy_in_Alaska "Energy in Alaska")—benefited greatly from the price inflation of the 1970s as did the [U.S. oil industry](/wiki/Petroleum_in_the_United_States "Petroleum in the United States") in general. Oil prices generally increased throughout the decade; between 1978 and 1980 the price of [West Texas Intermediate](/wiki/West_Texas_Intermediate "West Texas Intermediate") crude oil increased 250 percent.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Although all states felt the effects of the stock market crash and related national economic problems, the economic benefits of increased oil revenue in the Oil Patch states generally offset much of this.
